People who are familiar with the wonderful qualities of Christ and the comfort of having a relationship with him want to know where they can find him. Those who want to find Christ must seek him early and diligently.

Christ’s church is like a garden, protected and separated from the world; he takes care of it, enjoys it, and visits it. Those who want to find Christ should attend his services, including hearing the word, participating in sacraments, and praying. When Christ comes to his church, it is to spend time with his friends and take believers with him. He chooses the lilies one by one; and on the great day he will send out his angels to gather all his lilies so that he may be admired forever in them. The death of a believer is no more than an owner of a garden picking a favorite flower; and he will keep it from fading, yes, cause it to flourish forever with increasing beauty. If your own heart can confirm that you belong to Christ, do not question whether he belongs to you, because the agreement never fails on his side. It is comforting for the church to know that he feeds among the lilies, that he takes delight in his people.

All true excellence and holiness on earth are found in the church. Christ goes forward conquering his enemies, while his followers gain victories over the world, sinful desires, and the devil. He shows how gentle a Redeemer he is, how much joy he finds in his redeemed people, and how his grace works within them. Only true believers can possess the beauty of holiness. And when their true character is known, it will be praised. Both the church and believers look like the early morning at their first conversion, with a small but growing light. As for their sanctification, they are as beautiful as the moon, receiving all their light, grace, and holiness from Christ; and as for justification, clear as the sun, clothed with Christ, the Sun of righteousness, and fighting the good fight of faith under Christ’s banners against all spiritual enemies.

A Christian's character is formed and perfected through quiet reflection and meditation. But not in the quietness of someone who is lazy, self-indulgent, or frivolous. When a Christian is released from their duties in life, the world has no appeal to them. Their prayer is that all things relating to the Spirit may live and grow within them and around them. These are the interesting concerns and activities of one whom the world wrongly considers unhappy and lost to his true interests. In humility and self-abasement, a humble Christian would turn away from everything; but the Lord delights to honor him. Primarily, however, this refers to the ministering angels who will be sent for the soul of the Christian. Their approach may be startling, but the departing soul will find the Lord its strength and portion forever. The church is called Shulamite: the word means perfection and peace; not in herself, but in Christ, in whom she is complete through his righteousness; and has peace that he made for her through his blood, and gives to her by his Spirit.
